---
author: Tristan Muno
date: last-modified
date-format: MMMM D, YYYY
bibliography: ../references.bib
biblio-style: apsr
format:
  revealjs:
    embed-resources: true
    preview-links: false
    slideNumber: true
    incremental: true
    code-fold: true
    code-summary: "View source"
    pdfSeparateFragments: false
    footer: QMIR -- {{< meta date >}} -- Week 11
    logo: ./images/uma_ss.png
    theme: theme.scss
editor: source
execute:
  echo: true
  message: false
  warning: false
  freeze: true
  cache: true
---

```{r, message = NA}
#| label: setup
#| include: false
#| eval: true
start_time <- Sys.time()

library(tidyverse) # wrangling and visualization
library(brms) # Bayesian models via Stan
library(bayesplot) # MCMC diagnostics and posterior plots
library(patchwork) # combine ggplot panels
library(modelsummary) # regression tables

# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# SHARED COLOURS
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
col_1 <- "steelblue"
col_2 <- "#E07B39"
col_3 <- "#7B3F9E"
col_4 <- "goldenrod3"
chain_cols <- c(col_1, col_2, col_3, col_4)

# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# HELPER: Draw a simple DAG with ggplot2 (reused from Week 9)
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
draw_dag <- function(
  nodes,
  edges,
  title = NULL,
  xlim = c(0, 4),
  ylim = c(0.4, 3.2)
) {
  p <- ggplot() +
    geom_segment(
      data = edges,
      aes(x = x, y = y, xend = xend, yend = yend),
      arrow = arrow(length = unit(0.22, "cm"), type = "closed"),
      linewidth = 0.9,
      colour = "gray25",
      lineend = "butt"
    ) +
    geom_label(
      data = nodes,
      aes(x = x, y = y, label = label),
      size = 3.6,
      fill = "white",
      colour = "gray10",
      fontface = "bold",
      label.padding = unit(0.45, "lines"),
      label.r = unit(0.25, "lines")
    ) +
    coord_cartesian(xlim = xlim, ylim = ylim) +
    theme_void() +
    theme(
      plot.title = element_text(
        size = 11,
        hjust = 0.5,
        face = "bold",
        colour = "gray25",
        margin = margin(b = 6)
      )
    )
  if (!is.null(title)) {
    p <- p + labs(title = title)
  }
  p
}

# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# DATASET: Voter Turnout (Synthetic, n = 1000)
#
# True DGP (logit scale):
#   α = -0.2, β_pol = 0.12, β_income = 0.45, β_education = 0.55, β_gender = 0.20
#
# Confounders: income, education, gender drive both pol_interest and voted.
# Naive bivariate model overstates the pol_interest coefficient.
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
set.seed(2026)
n <- 1000

income <- rnorm(n, 0, 1)
education <- rnorm(n, 0, 1)
gender <- rbinom(n, 1, 0.5) # 0 = female, 1 = male

pol_interest_latent <- 5 +
  1.2 * income +
  0.8 * education +
  0.3 * gender +
  rnorm(n, 0, 1.2)
pol_interest <- as.integer(round(pmin(pmax(pol_interest_latent, 0), 10)))

eta <- -0.2 +
  0.12 * pol_interest +
  0.45 * income +
  0.55 * education +
  0.20 * gender
prob_voted <- plogis(eta)
voted <- rbinom(n, 1, prob_voted)

df <- tibble(voted, pol_interest, income, education, gender) |>
  mutate(gender_lab = if_else(gender == 1, "Male", "Female"))

rm(pol_interest_latent, eta, prob_voted)

# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# PRE-FIT MODELS (cached so slides render instantly)
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

# Prior predictive check only
fit_prior <- brm(
  voted ~ pol_interest + income + education + gender,
  data = df,
  family = bernoulli(),
  prior = c(
    prior(normal(0, 1.5), class = "Intercept"),
    prior(normal(0, 1), class = "b")
  ),
  sample_prior = "only",
  chains = 2,
  iter = 1000,
  warmup = 500,
  seed = 2026
)

# Naive bivariate
fit_naive <- brm(
  voted ~ pol_interest,
  data = df,
  family = bernoulli(),
  prior = c(
    prior(normal(0, 1.5), class = "Intercept"),
    prior(normal(0, 1), class = "b")
  ),
  chains = 4,
  iter = 2000,
  warmup = 1000,
  seed = 2026
)

# Adjusted (all confounders)
fit_adjusted <- brm(
  voted ~ pol_interest + income + education + gender,
  data = df,
  family = bernoulli(),
  prior = c(
    prior(normal(0, 1.5), class = "Intercept"),
    prior(normal(0, 1), class = "b")
  ),
  chains = 4,
  iter = 2000,
  warmup = 1000,
  seed = 2026
)
```

## QMIR 2026 {#sec-title}

::: notes
- Opening.
- Arc: first half (conceptual, ~55 min) + second half (live coding walkthrough, ~35 min).
- Core message: same machinery as Weeks 8–10; only the likelihood changes.
- The dataset is a voter-turnout simulation — political interest, income, education, gender, and a binary outcome.
:::

### Week 11: Modeling Binary Outcomes

![](./images/uma_palace.png){width="100%"} {{< meta author >}} <a href="https://github.com/dertristan" target="_blank" style="text-decoration: none; margin-left: 10px;"> <svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" width="20" height="20" fill="currentColor" class="bi bi-github" viewBox="0 0 16 16"> <path d="M8 0C3.58 0 0 3.58 0 8c0 3.54 2.29 6.53 5.47 7.59.4.07.55-.17.55-.38 0-.19-.01-.82-.01-1.49-2.01.37-2.53-.49-2.69-.94-.09-.23-.48-.94-.82-1.13-.28-.15-.68-.52-.01-.53.63-.01 1.08.58 1.23.82.72 1.21 1.87.87 2.33.66.07-.52.28-.87.51-1.07-1.78-.2-3.64-.89-3.64-3.95 0-.87.31-1.59.82-2.15-.08-.2-.36-1.02.08-2.12 0 0 .67-.21 2.2.82.64-.18 1.32-.27 2-.27s1.36.09 2 .27c1.53-1.04 2.2-.82 2.2-.82.44 1.1.16 1.92.08 2.12.51.56.82 1.27.82 2.15 0 3.07-1.87 3.75-3.65 3.95.29.25.54.73.54 1.48 0 1.07-.01 1.93-.01 2.2 0 .21.15.46.55.38A8.01 8.01 0 0 0 16 8c0-4.42-3.58-8-8-8"/> </svg> </a> <a href="mailto:tristan.muno@uni-mannheim.de" style="text-decoration: none; margin-left: 5px;"> <svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" width="20" height="20" fill="currentColor" class="bi bi-envelope" viewBox="0 0 16 16"> <path d="M0 4a2 2 0 0 1 2-2h12a2 2 0 0 1 2 2v8a2 2 0 0 1-2 2zm2-1a1 1 0 0 0-1 1v.217l7 4.2 7-4.2V4a1 1 0 0 0-1-1zm13 2.383-4.708 2.825L15 11.105zm-.034 6.876-5.64-3.471L8 9.583l-1.326-.795-5.64 3.47A1 1 0 0 0 2 13h12a1 1 0 0 0 .966-.741M1 11.105l4.708-2.897L1 5.383z"/> </svg> </a> <br> {{< meta date >}}

---

## Week 11 Learning Goals {#sec-goals .smaller}

::: notes
- Read aloud. Three goals map onto first half (Part 1, subparts 1.1–1.9) and second half (Part 2, subparts 2.1–2.12).
- Students already have the full Bayesian workflow from Weeks 8–10 — today they apply it to a new likelihood family.
- Goal 3 (LOO) is the first time LOO is formally explained from scratch — it appeared in Week 9 slides but was not delivered in class.
:::

By the end of today, you should be able to:

1. Explain why **OLS is structurally wrong for binary outcomes** and how the GLM framework resolves this via the Bernoulli likelihood and a link function
2. **Fit and interpret a Bayesian logistic regression** with `brms` — including priors on the logit scale, posterior coefficient summaries, and predicted probability ribbons
3. Assess model quality using **posterior predictive checks, confusion matrix, ROC/AUC, and LOO** — and compare models using Bayesian leave-one-out cross-validation

---

## Today's Core Message {#sec-core-message .smaller}

::: notes
- Say this explicitly. It is the most important frame for the session.
- "You have been doing Bayesian inference for three weeks. Nothing about the engine changes today. We are only swapping the likelihood."
- Then proceed to motivate WHY the Gaussian likelihood is wrong for binary data — that's the content of Part I.
:::

The Bayesian inferential machinery is **unchanged**:

$$
p(\mathbf{\theta} \mid \mathbf{Y}, \mathbf{X}) \;\propto\; L(\mathbf{Y}, \mathbf{X} \mid \mathbf{\theta}) \;\times\; p(\mathbf{\theta})
$$ {#eq-bayes-core}

. . .

**Weeks 8–10**: $L(\mathbf{Y}, \mathbf{X} \mid \boldsymbol{\theta}) = \prod_i \text{Normal}(Y_i \mid \mu_i, \sigma)$

**Week 11**: $L(\mathbf{Y}, \mathbf{X} \mid \boldsymbol{\theta}) = \prod_i \text{Bernoulli}(Y_i \mid \mu_i)$

. . .

The `brms` call changes by **one word**:

```r
brm(y ~ x, family = gaussian(), ...)   # Weeks 8–10
brm(y ~ x, family = bernoulli(), ...)  # Week 11
```

. . .

::: {.callout-note style="font-size: 0.78em"}
Before we can use the Bernoulli likelihood, we need to understand why the Gaussian likelihood fails for binary outcomes and how the GLM framework connects the two.
:::

# Part 1: Conceptual Foundations {#sec-part1}

## Why Not Just Use OLS? {#sec-ols-problem .smaller}

*1.1 Why OLS Fails for Binary Outcomes*

::: notes
- "The linear probability model (LPM) is OLS applied to a 0/1 outcome. It produces predicted values interpreted as probabilities. But it has three structural problems that are not fixable with better data or better priors."
- The problems are not a frequentist vs. Bayesian issue — a Bayesian model with `family = gaussian()` on binary data is equally broken.
- Key insight: these problems are properties of the Gaussian + linear model combination, not of any inferential framework.
:::

Apply OLS to a binary outcome $Y_i \in \{0, 1\}$: the **Linear Probability Model (LPM)**.

. . .

**Problem 1 — Out-of-range predictions**:

$$
\hat{\mu}_i = \alpha + \beta X_i \quad \text{is unbounded on } \mathbb{R}
$$ {#eq-lpm}

For extreme values of $X$, $\hat{\mu}_i \notin [0, 1]$ — a "probability" above 1 or below 0 is nonsensical.

. . .

**Problem 2 — Heteroskedasticity by construction**:

For a Bernoulli outcome, $\text{Var}(Y_i \mid X_i) = \mu_i(1 - \mu_i)$, which depends on $\mu_i$.
OLS assumes constant variance (Gauss-Markov A3); that assumption is structurally violated *regardless of the data*.

. . .

**Problem 3 — Residuals are not Normal**:

Binary $Y$ residuals take only two values ($1 - \hat{\mu}_i$ and $-\hat{\mu}_i$) — the Gaussian likelihood (A6) is mechanically wrong.

---

## The LPM in Practice {#sec-lpm-plot .smaller}

::: notes
- Show the scatter and ask students: "What happens at pol_interest = 0 or pol_interest = 10?"
- The OLS line exits [0, 1] on both sides — this is the visual proof of Problem 1.
- "A Bayesian model with family = gaussian() would produce exactly the same impossible predictions. The problem is the Gaussian + linear predictor combination, not the inferential framework."
:::

```{r}
#| label: fig-lpm
#| fig-cap: "OLS applied to a binary outcome: the linear fit exits [0, 1] for extreme predictor values."
#| dpi: 500
#| echo: true
fit_lpm <- lm(voted ~ pol_interest, data = df)

tibble(pol_interest = seq(-2, 13, by = 0.1)) |>
  mutate(pred = predict(fit_lpm, newdata = tibble(pol_interest))) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = pol_interest, y = pred)) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = c(0, 1), linetype = "dashed", colour = "gray50") +
  geom_jitter(
    data = df,
    aes(y = voted),
    height = 0.02,
    alpha = 0.12,
    colour = col_1,
    size = 0.9
  ) +
  geom_line(colour = col_2, linewidth = 1.3) +
  annotate(
    "text",
    x = 11,
    y = 1.06,
    label = "P > 1",
    colour = col_2,
    size = 3.5
  ) +
  annotate(
    "text",
    x = -1,
    y = -0.06,
    label = "P < 0",
    colour = col_2,
    size = 3.5
  ) +
  theme_bw() +
  labs(
    title = "Linear Probability Model: Predictions Exit [0, 1]",
    x = "Political Interest (0–10)",
    y = "Predicted P(voted = 1)"
  )
```

## Three Components of the GLM {#sec-glm-framework .smaller}

*1.2 The GLM Framework*

::: notes
- The GLM separates the linear predictor from the response distribution using a link function.
- "The link function is a translation layer. The linear predictor can roam freely over ℝ; the link squashes it back into (0, 1) before it becomes a probability."
- This resolves all three problems: (1) predictions are always in (0, 1); (2) variance is correct for Bernoulli; (3) likelihood is correctly specified.
:::

The **Generalized Linear Model** has three components:

. . .

**1. Linear predictor** — the familiar linear combination, unbounded on $\mathbb{R}$:

$$
\eta_i = \alpha + \beta_1 X_{i1} + \ldots + \beta_k X_{ik}
$$ {#eq-linear-predictor}

. . .

**2. Link function** $g(\cdot)$ — maps the linear predictor to the natural parameter of the response distribution.
For binary outcomes: $g(\mu_i) = \eta_i$, where $\mu_i = P(Y_i = 1 \mid X_i) \in (0, 1)$.

. . .

**3. Response distribution** — specifies how $Y_i$ is generated given $\mu_i$:

$$
Y_i \mid X_i \sim \text{Bernoulli}(\mu_i)
$$ {#eq-bernoulli-response}

---

## The Logistic Regression Model {#sec-logistic-model .smaller}

::: notes
- Put all three components together as a stack of equations.
- "The logistic function squashes any real number to (0, 1). If η_i = 0, μ_i = 0.5. Large positive η → μ near 1; large negative η → μ near 0. The S-shape is visible in the figure from Part I."
- Note that σ disappears: the Bernoulli has only one parameter. No separate noise term to estimate — variance is fully determined by the mean.
:::

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y_i &\sim \text{Bernoulli}(\mu_i) \\[4pt]
\mu_i &= \text{logistic}(\eta_i) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-\eta_i}} \\[4pt]
\eta_i &= \alpha + \beta_1 X_{i1} + \ldots + \beta_k X_{ik}
\end{aligned}
$$ {#eq-logistic-model}

. . .

::: {.callout-note style="font-size: 0.78em"}
**$\sigma$ disappears**: the Bernoulli distribution has only one free parameter ($\mu_i$).
Its variance $\mu_i(1 - \mu_i)$ is already fully determined by the mean — no separate noise term to estimate or place a prior on.
:::

. . .

**The link function as a translation layer**: the linear predictor $\eta_i$ roams freely over $\mathbb{R}$; the logistic function squashes it into $(0, 1)$ before it is used as a probability.
This resolves all three structural problems of the LPM.

## The Correct Likelihood for Binary Data {#sec-bernoulli-likelihood .smaller}

*1.3 The Bernoulli Likelihood*

::: notes
- Brief connection to Week 6: Bernoulli is Binomial(n=1, p). Students saw the Binomial when we simulated coin flips.
- Walk through the likelihood for one observation carefully. "If Y_i = 1, we want high μ_i to assign high probability. If Y_i = 0, we want low μ_i."
- The log-likelihood sum is what MCMC is maximising (weighted by the prior) — exact same structure as Weeks 8–10.
:::

The Bernoulli is the Binomial with $n = 1$: $\text{Binomial}(1, p) = \text{Bernoulli}(p)$.

. . .

::: {style="font-size: 0.72em"}
**Likelihood for one observation**:

$$
P(Y_i \mid \mu_i) = \mu_i^{Y_i}(1 - \mu_i)^{1 - Y_i}
$$ {#eq-bernoulli-one}

When $Y_i = 1$: $P = \mu_i$ (high $\mu$ is rewarded). When $Y_i = 0$: $P = 1 - \mu_i$.
:::

. . .

::: {style="font-size: 0.72em"}
**Full likelihood** (assuming independence across observations):

$$
L(\mathbf{Y}, \mathbf{X} \mid \boldsymbol{\beta}) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \mu_i^{Y_i}(1 - \mu_i)^{1 - Y_i}
$$ {#eq-bernoulli-full}
:::

. . .

::: {style="font-size: 0.72em"}
**Log-likelihood** (sum replaces product):

$$
\ell(\boldsymbol{\beta}) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left[ Y_i \log \mu_i + (1 - Y_i) \log(1 - \mu_i) \right]
$$ {#eq-bernoulli-loglik}

where $\mu_i = \text{logistic}(\eta_i)$.
The structure is identical to Weeks 8–10 — only the functional form of $L$ changes.
:::

## Two Link Functions {#sec-logit-probit .smaller}

::: {style="font-size: 0.72em"}
*1.4 Two Link Functions*

::: notes
- One slide, can be cut if time is short. Both link functions are valid; the choice rarely matters in practice.
- "probit × 1.6 ≈ logit" is a useful translator for reading applied papers that use probit.
- brms: family = bernoulli() uses logit by default; family = bernoulli(link = "probit") switches — no value judgment implied.
:::

Two common link functions for binary outcomes — both are valid:

| Link | $g(\mu)$ | Inverse $g^{-1}(\eta)$ | Distribution |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Logit** | $\log\frac{\mu}{1-\mu}$ | $\frac{1}{1+e^{-\eta}}$ (logistic CDF) | Logistic |
| **Probit** | $\Phi^{-1}(\mu)$ | $\Phi(\eta)$ (Normal CDF) | Standard Normal |
:::

. . .

```{r}
#| label: fig-link-functions
#| fig-cap: "Logistic and standard normal CDFs: both are S-shaped and map ℝ → (0, 1). The logistic CDF has slightly heavier tails."
#| fig-height: 2
#| dpi: 500
#| echo: false
tibble(eta = seq(-4, 4, length.out = 400)) |>
  mutate(
    Logit = plogis(eta),
    Probit = pnorm(eta)
  ) |>
  pivot_longer(c(Logit, Probit), names_to = "Link", values_to = "prob") |>
  ggplot(aes(x = eta, y = prob, colour = Link)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.3) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c(Logit = col_2, Probit = col_1)) +
  theme_bw() +
  labs(
    title = "Two Link Functions: Logistic vs. Probit CDF",
    x = "Linear predictor η",
    y = "P(Y = 1)"
  )
```

. . .

::: {style="font-size: 0.72em"}
- Both produce nearly identical results in practice — the choice rarely matters
- Rule of thumb: probit coefficients $\times 1.6 \approx$ logit coefficients (useful for reading applied papers)
- `family = bernoulli()` uses logit by default; `family = bernoulli(link = "probit")` switches
:::

## One Line Changes {#sec-bayes-continuity .smaller}

*1.5 Why Our Bayesian Machinery Handles This Seamlessly*

::: notes
- This should feel like relief, not novelty.
- "You already know how to do this. You have been doing it since Week 8. The engine is: posterior ∝ likelihood × prior, evaluated via MCMC. That engine does not change. We are only telling it to use a different likelihood."
- Note that σ drops out — Week 8 needed a prior on σ; Week 11 does not.
- brms compiles new Stan code automatically for the Bernoulli likelihood.
:::

**Week 8 model**:

$$
p(\alpha, \beta, \sigma \mid \mathbf{Y}, \mathbf{X}) \;\propto\; \underbrace{\prod_{i} \text{Normal}(Y_i \mid \mu_i, \sigma)}_{\text{Gaussian likelihood}} \;\times\; \underbrace{p(\alpha, \beta, \sigma)}_{\text{Priors}}
$$ {#eq-week8-model}

. . .

**Week 11 model**:

$$
p(\alpha, \boldsymbol{\beta} \mid \mathbf{Y}, \mathbf{X}) \;\propto\; \underbrace{\prod_{i} \text{Bernoulli}(Y_i \mid \mu_i)}_{\text{Bernoulli likelihood}} \;\times\; \underbrace{p(\alpha, \boldsymbol{\beta})}_{\text{Priors}}
$$ {#eq-week11-model}

. . .

The only change: **Gaussian → Bernoulli**.
MCMC samples from the same posterior structure; `brms` compiles Stan code for the new likelihood automatically.

```r
brm(y ~ x, family = gaussian(), ...)   # Weeks 8–10 — needs prior on σ
brm(y ~ x, family = bernoulli(), ...)  # Week 11 — σ drops out
```

## Why Flat Priors Are Dangerous Here {#sec-prior-danger .smaller}

*1.6 Priors on the Logit Scale*

::: notes
- Counter-intuitive but important. "A Normal(0, 10) prior on the intercept looks vague — it is 10 standard deviations wide. But on the probability scale it is anything but vague."
- logistic(10) ≈ 0.9999; logistic(-10) ≈ 0.0001. Almost all prior mass ends up near 0 and 1, with almost nothing near 0.5.
- "This is not a vague prior. It is a strong prior belief that the baseline outcome probability is extreme. That is almost never what we mean."
:::

Priors for logistic regression must be placed on the **logit (log-odds) scale**.

. . .

**Counter-intuitive fact**: a wide prior on the logit scale piles mass at the *extremes* of the probability scale:

::: {style="font-size: 0.82em"}
| Logit value $\eta$ | $P = \text{logistic}(\eta)$ |
|---|---|
| $-10$ | $\approx 0.0001$ |
| $-3$ | $\approx 0.05$ |
| $0$ | $= 0.50$ |
| $+3$ | $\approx 0.95$ |
| $+10$ | $\approx 0.9999$ |
:::

. . .

A $\text{Normal}(0, 10)$ prior on $\alpha$ covers logit values far beyond $\pm 3$ — mapping almost all prior mass to probabilities near 0 or 1, with very little weight near 0.5.
This is the **opposite of vague**.

---

## Recommended Weakly Informative Priors {#sec-prior-recommended .smaller}

::: notes
- Following Gelman et al. and the rstanarm/brms conventions.
- For the intercept: Normal(0, 1.5) covers the range (-3, +3) on the logit scale with 95% of the mass. logistic(±3) ≈ (0.05, 0.95). This is genuine prior uncertainty about the baseline probability — it does not prejudge the outcome.
- For slopes: Normal(0, 1) on a standardized predictor. ±2 log-odds per unit is already a very large effect on the probability scale. Standardize first when predictors have different scales.
- Calibration: "Show me the prior predictive distribution." We do this in the walkthrough.
:::

```{r}
#| label: fig-prior-logit
#| fig-cap: "Normal(0, 1.5) prior on the intercept: 95% of prior mass maps to P ∈ (0.05, 0.95) — genuine uncertainty, not prejudice toward extremes."
#| fig-height: 3.6
#| dpi: 500
#| echo: false
q025 <- qnorm(0.025, 0, 1.5)
q975 <- qnorm(0.975, 0, 1.5)

tibble(eta = seq(-5, 5, length.out = 600)) |>
  mutate(prob = plogis(eta)) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = eta, y = prob)) +
  geom_ribbon(
    data = ~ filter(.x, between(eta, q025, q975)),
    aes(ymin = 0, ymax = prob),
    fill = col_1,
    alpha = 0.25
  ) +
  geom_line(colour = col_2, linewidth = 1.3) +
  geom_vline(
    xintercept = c(q025, q975),
    linetype = "dashed",
    colour = "gray50"
  ) +
  geom_hline(
    yintercept = c(plogis(q025), plogis(q975)),
    linetype = "dashed",
    colour = "gray50"
  ) +
  annotate(
    "text",
    x = -4.5,
    y = 0.72,
    label = "Normal(0, 1.5) prior:\n95% interval ≈ (−2.94, +2.94)\n→ P ∈ (0.05, 0.95)",
    colour = col_1,
    size = 3.2,
    hjust = 0
  ) +
  theme_bw() +
  labs(
    title = "Normal(0, 1.5) Intercept Prior → Probability Scale",
    x = "Log-odds η (logit scale)",
    y = "P(Y = 1)"
  )
```

. . .

$$
\alpha \sim \text{Normal}(0,\; 1.5) \qquad \beta_j \sim \text{Normal}(0,\; 1)
$$ {#eq-recommended-priors}

Priors are **philosophical commitments**: $\text{Normal}(0, 1)$ on a slope says "most predictors have modest effects on the probability scale." This is more rigorous than a flat prior, not less.

. . .

::: {.callout-note style="font-size: 0.75em"}
**Probit scale**: the same caution applies to the probit link — priors must be on the probit (standard normal quantile) scale.
$\text{Normal}(0, 1)$ on the intercept is a natural starting point: $\Phi(\pm 1) \approx (0.16, 0.84)$ — similar coverage to $\text{Normal}(0, 1.5)$ on the logit scale.
$\text{Normal}(0, 0.5)$ on slopes is roughly analogous to $\text{Normal}(0, 1)$ for logit.
:::

## Posterior Predicted Probabilities {#sec-pred-probs .smaller}

*1.8 Predicted Probabilities and Posterior Uncertainty*

::: notes
- 1.7 note: coefficients are log-odds — sign and CI exclusion of zero are interpretable, magnitude is not.
- Mirror the Week 8 distinction between posterior_epred() and posterior_predict().
- "posterior_epred() gives you the expected probability for a given covariate profile — with full posterior uncertainty. This is the right tool for substantive interpretation."
- "posterior_predict() gives you sampled 0/1 outcomes — it draws Bernoulli(μ) for each posterior draw. Use it for pp_check() and confusion matrix computation."
- The visualization recipe is live-coded in Part 2 Step 2.10 using conditional_effects().
:::

::: {.callout-note style="font-size: 0.78em"}
**1.7 Interpreting Coefficients** — Coefficients are on the **log-odds scale**.
You can read: the **sign** (direction of association) and whether **zero is excluded** from the credible interval (evidence of a systematic association).
You cannot read: magnitude as a probability change — the marginal effect is non-linear and depends on the baseline probability.
Use predicted probabilities (below) for substantive interpretation.
:::

. . .

::: {style="font-size: 0.72em"}

Two distinct posterior quantities (mirror Week 8):
:::

. . .

::: {style="font-size: 0.72em"}
**`posterior_epred()`** → $E[Y \mid X] = P(Y=1 \mid X)$

The expected probability for a given covariate profile, averaged over posterior uncertainty.
This is the right tool for substantive interpretation.
:::

. . .

::: {style="font-size: 0.72em"}
**`posterior_predict()`** → sampled $0/1$ outcomes

Each posterior draw samples $\tilde{Y} \sim \text{Bernoulli}(\mu)$.
Use for posterior predictive checks and confusion matrix computation.
:::

. . .

::: {style="font-size: 0.72em"}
**Visualisation recipe** (live demo in Part 2 — subpart 2.10):

1. Call `conditional_effects()` with `effects = "pol_interest"` and `conditions` fixing confounders at reference values (`income = 0`, `education = 0`, `gender = 0`)
2. Pass the result to `plot()` — produces an S-shaped ribbon showing P(voted = 1 | X) with 95% credible interval

The S-shaped ribbon is the link function made visible: the dose-response with honest posterior uncertainty.
:::

## Posterior Predictive Checks for Binary Data {#sec-ppc .smaller}

*1.9 Model Assessment*

::: notes
- "pp_check() with type = 'bars' is the correct check for binary data. Density plots make no sense — there are only two possible values."
- "The dark bar is the observed proportion of voted = 1. The grey bars are the posterior replications. A well-calibrated model clusters the grey bars tightly around the dark bar."
- Segue: "We will do this live in Part X Step 7."
:::

For Bernoulli outcomes, `pp_check(fit, type = "bars")` shows a **bar chart** of observed vs. replicated proportion of $Y = 1$:

```r
pp_check(fit_adjusted, type = "bars", ndraws = 50)
```

. . .

- **Dark bar**: observed proportion of `voted = 1`
- **Grey bars**: proportions in posterior-replicated datasets

A well-calibrated model produces grey bars clustered tightly around the dark bar.

. . .

::: {.callout-important style="font-size: 0.78em"}
**Always use `type = "bars"` for binary outcomes.**
Density plots (`type = "dens"`) are nonsensical for discrete $\{0, 1\}$ data.
:::

---

## Confusion Matrix {#sec-confusion .smaller}

::: notes
- "The confusion matrix assesses classification performance at a specific threshold — not the underlying probabilistic model. We care primarily about the latter."
- "Changing the threshold (default = 0.5) moves along the tradeoff curve — which motivates the ROC curve."
- Quick definitions: accuracy, sensitivity, specificity. No need to dwell.
:::

At a decision threshold (default: 0.5), classify each predicted probability as $\hat{Y} \in \{0, 1\}$:

| | Predicted 0 | Predicted 1 |
|---|---|---|
| Observed 0 | True Negative (TN) | False Positive (FP) |
| Observed 1 | False Negative (FN) | True Positive (TP) |

. . .

- **Accuracy**: $(TP + TN) / n$
- **Sensitivity** (true positive rate): $TP / (TP + FN)$ — correctly identified voters
- **Specificity** (true negative rate): $TN / (TN + FP)$ — correctly identified non-voters

. . .

::: {style="font-size: 0.82em"}
The confusion matrix at threshold 0.5 is one point on a continuous tradeoff curve.
Changing the threshold trades sensitivity for specificity — which motivates the ROC curve.
:::

---

## ROC Curve and AUC {#sec-roc .smaller}

::: notes
- "The ROC curve shows the sensitivity/specificity tradeoff across ALL possible thresholds simultaneously. The confusion matrix gives you one point on this curve."
- "AUC measures how well the model RANKS individuals by predicted probability — it is threshold-agnostic."
- AUC = 0.5 is the diagonal (random classifier). AUC = 1.0 is perfect discrimination.
- "Comparing two models by AUC is more informative than comparing accuracy at a single threshold."
:::

The **ROC curve** visualises the sensitivity/specificity tradeoff across *all* classification thresholds simultaneously.

. . .

$$
\text{Sensitivity}(t) = \frac{TP(t)}{TP(t) + FN(t)}, \qquad \text{FPR}(t) = \frac{FP(t)}{FP(t) + TN(t)}
$$ {#eq-roc}

. . .

**AUC** (Area Under the Curve): single-number summary

::: {style="font-size: 0.82em"}
| AUC | Interpretation |
|---|---|
| 1.0 | Perfect discrimination |
| 0.8–0.9 | Excellent |
| 0.7–0.8 | Acceptable |
| 0.5 | No better than random |
:::

. . .

**Key insight**: AUC is threshold-agnostic — it measures how well the model *ranks* individuals by predicted probability.
Comparing two models by AUC is more informative than comparing accuracy at any single threshold.

---

## Bayesian LOO Cross-Validation {#sec-loo .smaller}

::: notes
- LOO was in Week 9 slides but not delivered in class. This is the first full formal explanation.
- Motivating problem: in-sample fit always rewards complexity. We want out-of-sample predictive performance.
- PSIS-LOO: approximate LOO from a SINGLE fitted model. Key selling point: fast, one call.
- Pareto k diagnostics: k > 0.7 flags unreliable observations. Worth mentioning briefly.
- Connection to AIC/BIC: LOO is more principled — directly estimates out-of-sample performance, no asymptotic approximations.
:::

**Motivating problem**: how do we compare `voted ~ pol_interest` vs. `voted ~ pol_interest + income + education + gender`?
In-sample fit always rewards more parameters — we want *out-of-sample* predictive performance.

. . .

**Ideal**: leave-one-out cross-validation — refit on $n-1$ observations, evaluate on the held-out one, sum over all $n$ hold-outs.
**Problem**: requires $n$ model refits — too slow for MCMC.

. . .

**PSIS-LOO** (@vehtari2017practical): approximate LOO from a *single* fitted model by reweighting existing posterior draws.
Pareto-smoothing stabilises the importance weights to make the approximation reliable.

. . .

- **`elpd_loo`**: estimated sum of out-of-sample log predictive densities — higher = better
- **Pareto $k$ diagnostics**: $k > 0.7$ flags observations where the approximation is unreliable
- **Meaningful difference**: $\Delta\text{elpd} \gtrsim 2 \times \text{SE}_\Delta$

. . .

::: {.callout-note style="font-size: 0.75em"}
**Same `loo()` call for all `brms` models** — Gaussian, Bernoulli, multilevel.
This is the key selling point: one tool, one workflow, all model types.
Students will use it again in Week 12.
:::

# Part 2: Applied Walkthrough — Voter Turnout {#sec-part10}

## Research Question and Causal Structure {#sec-rq .smaller}

*2.1 Research Question and Causal Structure*

*Workflow Step 1 — DAG + Estimand*

::: notes
- State the research question clearly. The DAG tells us what to control for.
- "All three confounders drive both political interest and turnout simultaneously. If we don't adjust for them, the naive coefficient absorbs their influence and overstates the causal effect of political interest."
- The naive-to-adjusted comparison is the substantive payoff at the end.
:::

> **Does political interest increase the probability of voting? Does this effect persist after adjusting for socioeconomic confounders?**

```
income ────────────────────────┐
          ↘                    ↓
education ──→ pol_interest ──→ voted
          ↗                    ↑
gender ────────────────────────┘
```

---

## 2.2 Data Summary {#sec-step1 .smaller}

*Workflow Step 2 — Data Summary*

::: notes
- Point out the binary outcome — only 0s and 1s. Any other value would be a data problem.
- Show the turnout rate: roughly 65% voted. Realistic for a national election.
- The bar chart shows the positive raw correlation between political interest and turnout — before adjusting for confounders.
:::

```{r}
#| label: fig-step1-turnout
#| fig-cap: "Turnout rate by political interest level. A positive raw correlation is visible — but note this is unadjusted for income, education, and gender."
#| fig-height: 5
#| dpi: 500
df |> count(voted) |> mutate(prop = n / sum(n))

df |>
  ggplot(aes(x = pol_interest, fill = factor(voted))) +
  geom_bar(position = "fill") +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c(col_1, col_2),
    labels = c("Did not vote", "Voted"),
    name = NULL
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent) +
  theme_bw() +
  labs(
    title = "Turnout Rate by Political Interest Level",
    x = "Political Interest (0–10)",
    y = "Proportion"
  )
```

---

## 2.4 Prior Predictive Check {#sec-step3 .smaller}

*Workflow Step 4 — Prior Predictive Check*

::: notes
- "Before fitting to data, we ask: do our priors produce plausible turnout rates?"
- Normal(0, 1.5) on the intercept + Normal(0, 1) on all slopes → prior predictive turnout rates spread across [0, 1] without piling up at 0 or 1. That's what we want.
- "If the prior predictive check had produced all-zero or all-one predictions, that would be a red flag — our priors would be pushing the model toward an implausible baseline."
:::

Before fitting, verify that the chosen priors produce **plausible turnout rates**.
Use `sample_prior = "only"` to draw from the prior predictive distribution:

```{r}
#| label: code-fit-prior
#| eval: false
#| echo: false
fit_prior <- brm(
  voted ~ pol_interest + income + education + gender,
  data = df,
  family = bernoulli(),
  prior = c(
    prior(normal(0, 1.5), class = "Intercept"),
    prior(normal(0, 1), class = "b")
  ),
  sample_prior = "only",
  chains = 2,
  iter = 1000,
  warmup = 500,
  seed = 2026
)
```

```{r}
#| label: fig-step3-prior-ppc
#| fig-cap: "Prior predictive check: Normal(0, 1.5) intercept + Normal(0, 1) slopes produce prior turnout rates spread across [0, 1] — genuine prior uncertainty, not prejudice toward extreme probabilities."
#| fig-height: 5
#| dpi: 500
pp_check(fit_prior, type = "bars", ndraws = 50) +
  theme_bw() +
  labs(
    title = "Prior Predictive Check: Does the Prior Produce Plausible Turnout?"
  )
```

---

## 2.5 Fit Naive Model {#sec-step4 .smaller}

*Workflow Step 5 — Fit with brms*

::: notes
- "This is the same brm() workflow you know from Weeks 8–10. The only change is family = bernoulli()."
- The model is already pre-fit in the setup chunk — rendering is instant.
- Callout: #| cache: true is essential. Without it, every render refits Stan from scratch (30-60 seconds each time).
:::

```{r}
#| label: code-fit-naive
#| eval: false
fit_naive <- brm(
  voted ~ pol_interest,
  data = df,
  family = bernoulli(),
  prior = c(
    prior(normal(0, 1.5), class = "Intercept"),
    prior(normal(0, 1), class = "b")
  ),
  chains = 4,
  iter = 2000,
  warmup = 1000,
  seed = 2026
)
```

::: {.callout-tip style="font-size: 0.78em"}
**Always set `#| cache: true` on `brm()` chunks.**
Without caching, every document render refits the model from scratch (30–60 seconds each).
With caching, Quarto stores the result and only reruns the chunk when the code changes.
Add `_cache/` to your `.gitignore` — cache folders are large and machine-specific.
:::

---

## 2.6 Fit Adjusted Model {#sec-step5 .smaller}

*Workflow Step 5 — Fit with brms*

::: notes
- "One more predictor, same workflow. The prior on class = 'b' applies to all slope coefficients simultaneously."
- The conceptual payoff: naive vs. adjusted coefficient comparison in Step 8.
:::

```{r}
#| label: code-fit-adjusted
#| eval: false
fit_adjusted <- brm(
  voted ~ pol_interest + income + education + gender,
  data = df,
  family = bernoulli(),
  prior = c(
    prior(normal(0, 1.5), class = "Intercept"),
    prior(normal(0, 1), class = "b")
  ),
  chains = 4,
  iter = 2000,
  warmup = 1000,
  seed = 2026
)
```

. . .

::: {style="font-size: 0.82em"}
The `prior(normal(0, 1), class = "b")` applies to **all** slope coefficients.
If you want coefficient-specific priors, use the `coef =` argument:

```r
prior(normal(0, 0.5), class = "b", coef = "pol_interest")
```

For unstandardised predictors with very different scales, scale the prior to the predictor range.
:::

---

## 2.7 MCMC Diagnostics {#sec-step6 .smaller}

*Workflow Step 6a — MCMC Diagnostics*

::: notes
- plot() on a brms object is the recommended diagnostic workflow — trace + posterior density in one call.
- Walk through: "Four chains, well-mixed trace plots (no trends, no divergence between chains). Posterior densities are smooth and unimodal."
- summary() gives the numerical diagnostics: R-hat < 1.01 and Bulk ESS > 1000 for all parameters.
:::

`plot()` on a `brms` object produces combined **trace + posterior density** panels for every parameter:

```{r}
#| label: fig-step6-diagnostics
#| fig-cap: "MCMC diagnostics for fit_adjusted: trace plots (left) and posterior densities (right). Four chains should mix well — no trends, no divergence."
#| fig-height: 8.5
plot(fit_adjusted)
```

. . .

Check numerical diagnostics:

```{r}
#| label: step6-summary
#| eval: false
summary(fit_adjusted) # check R-hat < 1.01, Bulk ESS > 1000
```

---

## 2.8 Posterior Predictive Check {#sec-step7 .smaller}

*Workflow Step 6b — PPC*

::: notes
- "For Bernoulli models, always use type = 'bars'. Density plots don't make sense for binary data."
- Dark bar = observed proportion of voted = 1. Grey bars = posterior replications.
- A well-calibrated model clusters the grey bars around the dark bar.
:::

```{r}
#| label: fig-step7-ppc
#| fig-cap: "Posterior predictive check: light blue bars show the empirical distribution of outcomes; dark blue points and segments show predicted outcomes from 50 posterior draws (with 95% uncertainty)."
#| fig-height: 3.2
#| dpi: 500
pp_check(fit_adjusted, type = "bars", ndraws = 50) +
  theme_bw() +
  labs(title = "Posterior Predictive Check: Proportion Voted")
```

---

## 2.9 Coefficient Table {#sec-step8 .smaller}

*Workflow Step 7 — Interpretation*

::: notes
- Key observation: the pol_interest coefficient shrinks substantially in the adjusted model.
- "The DAG told us this would happen: income, education, and gender are confounders. The naive model attributed their shared influence to political interest."
- Remind students: coefficients are in log-odds. They are honest but not directly interpretable as probability changes — that's the purpose of the predicted probability plot in Step 9.
:::

```{r}
#| label: tbl-step8-coefs
#| tbl-cap: "Posterior coefficient summaries (log-odds scale) with 95% credible intervals. The pol_interest coefficient shrinks substantially from the naive to the adjusted model — confirming the DAG's confounding prediction."
modelsummary(
  list("Naive" = fit_naive, "Adjusted" = fit_adjusted),
  statistic = "conf.int",
  shape = term ~ model + statistic
)
```

. . .

::: {.callout-important style="font-size: 0.78em"}
**What you CAN read from this table:**

- **Sign**: direction of association (positive = higher X → higher probability of Y = 1)
- **Zero excluded from 95% credible interval**: evidence of a systematic association in that direction

**What you CANNOT read:**

- **Magnitude as probability change**: the marginal effect is non-linear — a coefficient of 0.12 does NOT mean "0.12 pp increase in turnout probability per unit of political interest"
- **Effect size in any intuitive unit**: use predicted probabilities (subpart 2.10) for substantive interpretation
:::

---

## 2.10 Predicted Probabilities {#sec-step9 .smaller}

*Workflow Step 7 — Interpretation*

::: notes
- "Hold the confounders at reference values. Female baseline (gender = 0) is the natural reference."
- "The S-shape is the link function made visible. The ribbon is the 95% posterior credible interval — it narrows where we have dense data and widens at the extremes."
- "conditional_effects() handles the grid construction and posterior averaging automatically. This is how you communicate the effect of political interest to a non-statistical audience — not with log-odds."
:::

```{r}
#| label: fig-step9-ribbon
#| fig-cap: "Predicted probability of voting as a function of political interest (income = 0, education = 0, gender = Female). Ribbon = 95% posterior credible interval from conditional_effects()."
#| fig-height: 5
#| dpi: 500
brms_condeff <- conditional_effects(
  fit_adjusted,
  effects = "pol_interest",
  conditions = data.frame(income = 0, education = 0, gender = 0),
  method = "posterior_epred",
  prob = 0.95
)

plot(brms_condeff, plot = FALSE)[[1]] +
  labs(x = "Political Interest Index")
```

::: {.callout-note style="font-size: 0.78em"}
`conditional_effects()` constructs the predictor grid, averages over posterior draws, and returns a 95% credible ribbon — the S-shaped curve is the link function made visible.
Fix confounders at reference values via `conditions`; vary the predictor of interest via `effects`.
:::

---

## 2.11 Confusion Matrix and ROC {#sec-step10 .smaller}

*Workflow Step 7 — Interpretation*

::: notes
- Walk through: the confusion matrix is ONE point on the ROC curve (at threshold 0.5).
- AUC tells us how well the model ranks individuals by predicted probability — threshold-agnostic.
- No additional packages needed: map_dfr (purrr, part of tidyverse) + base R arithmetic.
:::

```{r}
#| label: step10-confusion
pred_probs <- posterior_epred(fit_adjusted) |> apply(2, median)
pred_binary <- as.integer(pred_probs >= 0.5)
table(Predicted = pred_binary, Observed = df$voted)
```

```{r}
#| label: fig-step10-roc
#| fig-cap: "ROC curve for fit_adjusted. Each point = one classification threshold. The confusion matrix at threshold 0.5 is one point on this curve. AUC = area under curve (0.5 = random; 1.0 = perfect)."
#| fig-height: 3.0
#| dpi: 500
thresholds <- seq(0, 1, by = 0.01)
roc_df <- map_dfr(thresholds, function(t) {
  pred <- as.integer(pred_probs >= t)
  tp <- sum(pred == 1 & df$voted == 1)
  fp <- sum(pred == 1 & df$voted == 0)
  tn <- sum(pred == 0 & df$voted == 0)
  fn <- sum(pred == 0 & df$voted == 1)
  tibble(threshold = t, sensitivity = tp / (tp + fn), fpr = fp / (fp + tn))
})

auc_val <- roc_df |>
  arrange(fpr) |>
  summarise(
    auc = sum(diff(fpr) * (head(sensitivity, -1) + tail(sensitivity, -1)) / 2)
  ) |>
  pull(auc)

roc_df |>
  ggplot(aes(x = fpr, y = sensitivity)) +
  geom_line(colour = col_2, linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_abline(linetype = "dashed", colour = "gray50") +
  annotate(
    "text",
    x = 0.6,
    y = 0.25,
    label = paste0("AUC = ", round(auc_val, 3)),
    colour = col_1,
    size = 4.5
  ) +
  theme_bw() +
  labs(
    title = "ROC Curve: Adjusted Turnout Model",
    subtitle = "Each point = one classification threshold",
    x = "False Positive Rate (1 – Specificity)",
    y = "True Positive Rate (Sensitivity)"
  )
```

---

## 2.12 LOO Model Comparison {#sec-step11 .smaller}

*Workflow Step 7 — Interpretation*

::: notes
- First live use of LOO. Students now have the full explanation from subpart 1.9.
- Walk through the output: (1) per-model elpd_loo; (2) Pareto k diagnostics; (3) comparison table.
- "The adjusted model should score higher — consistent with the DAG's implication that the confounders are genuine."
- Continuity callout: same call again in Week 12 for pooled vs. partially pooled models.
:::

```{r}
#| label: step11-loo
loo(fit_naive, fit_adjusted)
```

. . .

Reading the output:

::: {style="font-size: 0.82em"}
1. **`elpd_loo`** per model — higher = better out-of-sample predictive performance
2. **Pareto $k$ diagnostics** — flag any $k > 0.7$ (unreliable approximation for that observation)
3. **Comparison table** — `elpd_diff` and `se_diff`: meaningful when $|\text{elpd_diff}| \gtrsim 2 \times \text{se_diff}$
:::

. . .

::: {.callout-note style="font-size: 0.75em"}
**Continuity**: same `loo()` call, same interpretation, in Week 12 — comparing pooled vs. partially pooled multilevel models.
:::

# Summary {#sec-summary}

## The Canonical 8-Step Workflow {#sec-workflow .smaller}

::: notes
- Read through. This workflow applies regardless of likelihood family.
- "Every analysis you submit must have these eight elements — bivariate, multivariate, interaction, logistic (today), multilevel (Week 12)."
- The likelihood changes; the workflow does not.
:::

The same eight-step workflow applies to **every** model:

. . .

::: {style="font-size: 0.7em"}
| Step | What you do | Why |
|------|------------|-----|
| 1. DAG + Estimand | Draw the causal graph; state the quantity of interest | Clarifies what to estimate and what to control for |
| 2. Data Summary | Visualise and describe your variables | Check for patterns, outliers, and model plausibility |
| 3. Formal Model | Write out $L$ and all priors before coding | Forces precision; makes assumptions explicit |
| 4. Prior Predictive | Simulate $Y$ from priors; check plausibility | Catches implausible priors before MCMC |
| 5. Fit with `brms` | Run `brm()` with explicit priors | Obtains posterior draws |
| 6a. MCMC Diagnostics | Check $\hat{R}$, ESS, trace plots | Confirms convergence before interpreting |
| 6b. PPC | Run `pp_check()` | Checks model adequacy |
| 7. Interpretation | Posterior summaries, predicted probabilities, LOO | Answers the research question |
| 8. Limitations | State what could still bias results | Scientific honesty |
:::

---

## Learning Goals: Check {#sec-goals-check .smaller}

::: notes
- Read through quickly. Connect each goal to where it was covered.
- Week 12 preview: hierarchical data, random intercepts and slopes, partial pooling.
:::

::: {style="font-size: 0.82em"}
By the end of today, you should be able to:

1. ✓ Explain why OLS is structurally wrong for binary outcomes (out-of-range predictions, heteroskedasticity, non-Normal residuals) and how the GLM framework resolves this via Bernoulli likelihood + link function (subparts 1.1–1.4)

2. ✓ Fit and interpret a Bayesian logistic regression with `brms` — including weakly informative priors on the logit scale, posterior coefficient summaries, and predicted probability ribbons (subparts 1.5–1.8, steps 2.5–2.10)

3. ✓ Assess model quality using posterior predictive checks, confusion matrix, ROC/AUC, and LOO — and compare models using Bayesian leave-one-out cross-validation (subpart 1.9, steps 2.8–2.12)
:::

. . .

::: {.callout-important style="font-size: 0.78em"}
**Homework (due before next session)**: Apply the full 8-step workflow to a new dataset, fitting and interpreting a Bayesian logistic regression. GitHub Classroom link on the course website.
:::

---

## Course Evaluation Survey {#sec-survey .smaller}

Your feedback matters — it shapes this course and future iterations.

. . .

Please take 5 minutes at the end of today (or this week) to complete the **official course evaluation survey**.

. . .

The survey is **anonymous** and covers:

- Quality of teaching and materials
- Pace and difficulty level
- What worked well and what could be improved

. . .

::: {.callout-note style="font-size: 0.82em"}
Honest, constructive feedback — including critical feedback — is genuinely appreciated.
The evaluation results are shared with the department and influence course design.
:::

---

## Bridge to Week 12 {#sec-bridge .smaller}

::: notes
- "Next week we let coefficients vary by group. The Bayesian machinery is the same — we add a hierarchical prior that pools information across groups."
- Students will use loo() again in Week 12 to compare pooled vs. partially pooled models.
- Partial pooling is a direct consequence of the Bayesian hierarchical prior structure — they have the conceptual tools already.
:::

**Next week: Multilevel Models and Partial Pooling**

. . .

::: {style="font-size: 0.82em"}
The structure of next week mirrors today exactly — same workflow, different likelihood component:

- Data with a **hierarchical structure**: individuals nested within countries (or students within universities, observations within regions)
- **Random intercepts**: allow the baseline outcome probability to vary by group
- **Partial pooling**: group-level estimates are pulled toward the population mean — a direct consequence of the Bayesian hierarchical prior
- **LOO again**: compare pooled vs. partially pooled models using the same `loo()` call from Step 11
:::

. . .

::: {.callout-note style="font-size: 0.78em"}
**The conceptual core is the same**: likelihood + prior → posterior via MCMC.
We add one new ingredient — a prior distribution *over the group-level intercepts* themselves.
:::

## References {#sec-references}

```{r}
#| label: session-info
#| eval: true
#| include: false
sessionInfo()
```

```{r}
#| label: exec-time
#| eval: true
#| include: false
Sys.time() - start_time
```
